name: Traditional Built-In Five-Part Symmetrical Display and Storage Cabinet with Moulded Central Divider Pilaster
description: A large, full-height traditional built-in display and storage cabinet conceived and executed as architectural joinery rather than freestanding furniture. The overall composition is strongly symmetrical and reads as a five-part frontage across its full width: a full-height left outer flanking tower, a left inner display and storage bay, a single prominent full-height central vertical divider pilaster, a right inner display and storage bay, and a full-height right outer flanking tower. The entire assembly is capped by a continuous classical crown and cornice moulding running the full width at the top and is grounded by a continuous base plinth at floor level. The five-part arrangement is the primary organisational logic of the piece and is the definitive structural interpretation reconciling both the sketch evidence and the text brief. The upper zones of the two inner bays are dominated by open display shelving arranged as a dense, grid-like matrix of adjustable shelves and vertical sub-dividers creating individual display niches and cubbies of varying sizes, consistent with a traditional dresser hutch or bibliothèque arrangement for china, books, and decorative objects. The lower zones of the two inner bays transition to a symmetrical bank of drawers — six drawer fronts in total, arranged as two stacks of three across the two inner sections, with traditional bail or cup pull hardware. A fixed mid-level horizontal structural shelf and counter rail at approximately 85–95 cm from floor level marks the transition between the upper open display zone and the lower enclosed storage zone; this element is integrated into the carcass as a structural fixed shelf and, in the recommended stepped-depth configuration, projects as a visible counter ledge of approximately 5–8 cm beyond the face of the upper display section. The two flanking outer tower sections each feature open shelving in the upper portion and a single hinged panel door in the lower portion. The defining design element of the entire piece — explicitly mandated by the text brief — is the single central full-height vertical divider pilaster positioned at the exact centreline of the cabinet, running from the top of the base plinth to the underside of the cornice. Both the left and right vertical edges of this panel carry continuously routed classical moulding profiles running the full height: ogee, cove, bead and astragal, and fillet transitions, approximately 20–40 mm wide and 8–15 mm deep per edge, giving the panel the character of a classical pilaster or architectural column strip. This is the primary decorative character element of the piece and must be explicitly detailed in all production drawings with a full cross-section profile. The cabinet reads as formal, symmetrical, classically composed architectural built-in joinery integrated into a wall, consistent with Georgian, Victorian, or French Provincial period cabinetry traditions. The crown moulding is a stepped traditional profile with fascia, bed mould, and cap. The base plinth is continuous and moisture-protected at the floor junction. All sections carry full recessed back panels. The piece is fixed mechanically to the wall and floor as architectural joinery.

dimensions.depth: 42 cm lower storage zone and flanking towers (uniform body depth); 35–38 cm upper inner display zone (stepped back to create counter ledge at mid-level shelf); crown cornice projects to approximately 46–50 cm at top
dimensions.notes: Width is set at 220 cm — the maximum of the text brief's explicitly stated 180–220 cm range — as the most defensible compromise that respects the brief while allowing workable proportions for the five-part composition. At 220 cm total, indicative section widths are: each flanking outer tower approximately 28–32 cm wide; each inner display and storage bay approximately 55–65 cm wide; central divider pilaster approximately 15–20 cm wide. Height of 230 cm is consistent with the full-height built-in character shown in the sketch and is appropriate for domestic ceiling heights of 240–270 cm, allowing for crown scribe or small infill above. The recommended depth configuration is stepped: lower storage zone and flanking towers at 420 mm deep; upper inner display shelving reduced to 350–380 mm deep; this creates a functional counter ledge of approximately 40–70 mm at the mid-level structural shelf at approximately 85–95 cm above floor. Crown cornice projection extends overall depth locally to approximately 460–500 mm at the top. If the client prefers a uniform depth throughout, 420 mm uniform is acceptable but eliminates the counter ledge. SKETCH CONFLICT NOTE: The sketch's five-bay proportional composition implies a cabinet width of approximately 260–320 cm if drawn to realistic scale. The text brief constrains width to 180–220 cm. The text brief takes precedence. Client confirmation is required if sketch proportions are intended to be dimensionally accurate, in which case width should be revised upward and all section widths recalculated. Lower drawer zone approximately 35–45 cm tall above plinth. Mid-level shelf at approximately 85–95 cm from floor. Upper open shelving zone approximately 100–115 cm tall. Cornice height approximately 10–15 cm. Base plinth approximately 10–15 cm tall.

style.aesthetic: Traditional classical built-in architectural joinery — formal, symmetrical, period-accurate fitted cabinetry with pilaster and entablature detailing
style.era: Georgian and Victorian inspired with French Provincial references — European classical cabinetmaking tradition, 18th to 19th century
influences: Georgian architectural millwork and alcove cabinetry
Victorian fitted library and dining room joinery
French Provincial armoire, bibliothèque, and dresser traditions
Classical pilaster, column, and entablature detailing
English period dresser hutch and breakfront bookcase traditions
Architectural built-in cabinetry — alcove cabinets and chimney breast joinery

materials.name: 18 mm furniture-grade MDF or veneered hardwood plywood
materials.usage: Primary carcass construction — side gables, internal bay partitions, fixed and adjustable shelves under 500 mm span, cabinet tops and bottoms, drawer carcass panels, back panel substrate
materials.finish: Option A (primary recommendation): Painted satin enamel in off-white or warm cream. Option B (secondary): Stained and satin-lacquered hardwood veneer in warm oak, walnut, or mahogany tone. Both options are valid; painted finish is the primary specification for classical architectural built-in character.

materials.name: 25 mm solid hardwood or built-up veneered board
materials.usage: Central divider pilaster face and body; mid-level structural shelf and counter rail; weight-bearing fixed shelves spanning over 500 mm; visible front framing members; thickened long-span display shelves; countertop surface at mid-level ledge; bottom panels of flanking towers
materials.finish: Identical finish to carcass throughout — fully integrated, not contrasting. Solid hardwood edge banding on all exposed edges.

materials.name: Solid hardwood
materials.usage: Applied and routed classical moulding profiles — crown and cornice built-up assembly (fascia, bed mould, cap); base plinth moulding cap; central divider pilaster edge profiles (ogee, cove, bead and astragal, fillet) routed or applied continuously along both vertical edges full height; face frame stiles and rails; shelf front lippings; drawer fronts in traditional raised or flat panel style; door frames and raised panel door construction for lower flanking tower cupboards; all exposed decorative moulding runs
materials.finish: Painted or stained and lacquered to match carcass body — fully integrated finish throughout

materials.name: 6 mm veneered plywood or 3 mm HDF
materials.usage: Recessed back panels covering the full interior back of all five sections — display shelving bays, drawer zones, and lower cupboard interiors
materials.finish: Painted to match interior finish; display section backs may optionally be painted a slightly deeper tone or lined with fabric or wallpaper for visual depth behind displayed objects

materials.name: 12–15 mm birch plywood
materials.usage: Drawer boxes — sides, base, and back of all drawer carcasses

materials.name: Moisture-resistant MDF or treated hardwood-faced plywood
materials.usage: Base plinth panels at floor level — moisture protection at floor and plinth junction for built-in installation
materials.finish: Painted to match; sealant applied at floor junction

materials.name: Steel flat bar (optional reinforcement)
materials.usage: Hidden reinforcement routed into the underside of long horizontal shelf spans exceeding 700 mm to prevent sag — particularly the mid-level structural shelf and long upper display shelves
materials.finish: Fully concealed within shelf build-up — not visible in finished piece

materials.name: Metal hardware
materials.usage: Concealed soft-close cup hinges for all doors; full-extension undermount soft-close drawer slides for all drawers; adjustable shelf support pins in 5 mm holes on 32 mm centres for all adjustable shelves; wall anchors and mounting brackets for structural wall fixing; traditional antique brass bail pulls or cup pulls for all drawer fronts; antique brass cup pulls or knobs for lower cupboard doors
materials.finish: Antique brass or aged bronze throughout for period consistency — all hardware to match. Push-to-open hardware is a valid contemporary alternative for lower tower doors if a clean hardware-free aesthetic is preferred — client decision point.

materials.name: Clear glass (optional)
materials.usage: Possible glazing for upper central door pair if that element is confirmed by client — timber-framed glazed doors in the upper central zone adjacent to the central divider
materials.finish: Clear polished glass in solid hardwood frames, finished to match cabinet body

colors.primary: Off-white or warm cream — e.g. Farrow and Ball All White No. 2005, Pointing No. 2003, or Wimborne White No. 239; or equivalent trade satin enamel. Most period-appropriate for painted Georgian and French Provincial architectural joinery.
colors.secondary: Natural hardwood tone — warm oak, walnut, or mahogany with satin lacquer — if stained finish is selected as the alternative to painted. Central divider and all moulded elements finished identically to the rest of the front elevation in either option.
colors.accent: Antique brass or aged bronze — drawer bail pulls or cup pulls, door cup pulls or knobs, butt hinges or concealed cup hinges. Consistent and period-correct throughout.
colors.notes: Painted finish in off-white or warm cream is the primary recommendation and the most historically aligned choice for classical fitted joinery. Interior of display shelving may optionally be painted one shade deeper than the exterior body or lined with fabric or wallpaper for visual depth and contrast behind displayed objects. All hardware must be consistent in finish throughout the piece — antique brass is the period-correct primary specification. A stained natural wood version is a valid secondary option and will read as formal library or period furniture cabinetry.

structural_elements.name: Base Plinth
structural_elements.description: Continuous traditional floor plinth running the full width of the cabinet at floor level, supporting the entire built-in assembly. Slightly recessed from the cabinet face to create a toe-kick shadow line. Integrates with room skirting boards. Moisture-protected at the floor junction with sealant. Capped with a solid hardwood moulding profile on the front face. Plinth height approximately 10–15 cm.
structural_elements.material: Moisture-resistant MDF or treated hardwood-faced plywood carcass with solid hardwood moulding cap on front face

structural_elements.name: Crown and Cornice Moulding
structural_elements.description: Continuous projecting classical crown and cornice assembly running the full width of the cabinet at the top, completing the architectural composition. Stepped traditional profile comprising fascia board, bed mould, and cap moulding built up from individual solid hardwood moulding runs over an MDF or plywood substrate. Projects forward of the cabinet face by approximately 40–80 mm. Scribed to ceiling or soffit condition at installation, or a small painted infill panel used above if ceiling height exceeds cabinet height. Cornice height approximately 10–15 cm. Crown projection extends overall depth locally to approximately 460–500 mm.
structural_elements.material: Solid hardwood moulding profiles built up over MDF or plywood substrate, painted or stained to match

structural_elements.name: Left Flanking Outer Tower
structural_elements.description: Full-height narrow tower section at the far left of the composition, framing the left edge of the cabinet. Upper portion contains open shelving with 2–3 adjustable shelves on shelf pins in 5 mm holes on 32 mm centres, backed by a full recessed back panel. Lower portion contains a single hinged solid raised-panel door in traditional frame-and-panel construction with antique brass cup pull or knob and concealed soft-close cup hinges; lower interior is an enclosed cupboard with a fixed shelf. A fixed 25 mm structural shelf at approximately 90–100 cm from floor divides the upper open shelving zone from the lower cupboard zone and acts as the door stop reference. Corner bracket or base moulding articulation detail at the tower base. Face frame with classical moulding detailing on stiles and rails. Full back panel throughout upper and lower zones. Tower width approximately 28–32 cm.
structural_elements.material: 18 mm MDF or plywood carcass with 25 mm fixed dividing shelf, solid hardwood face frame, door frame and raised panel door construction, solid hardwood moulding details, 6 mm back panel
structural_elements.position: Far left, full height from base plinth to underside of cornice

structural_elements.name: Right Flanking Outer Tower
structural_elements.description: Exact mirror image of the left flanking outer tower. Full-height narrow tower at the far right of the composition. Upper open shelving with 2–3 adjustable shelves, lower single hinged solid raised-panel door cupboard. Fixed 25 mm structural dividing shelf at approximately 90–100 cm from floor. Identical construction, detailing, moulding profiles, and hardware to the left tower. Full symmetry confirmed. Tower width approximately 28–32 cm.
structural_elements.material: 18 mm MDF or plywood carcass with 25 mm fixed dividing shelf, solid hardwood face frame, door frame and raised panel door construction, solid hardwood moulding details, 6 mm back panel
structural_elements.position: Far right, full height from base plinth to underside of cornice

structural_elements.name: Central Vertical Divider Pilaster
structural_elements.description: THE DEFINING DESIGN ELEMENT — explicitly mandated by the text brief. A single prominent full-height vertical panel positioned at the exact centreline of the cabinet, running from the top of the base plinth to the underside of the cornice. Panel width approximately 15–20 cm; depth matching the full cabinet body depth. Both the left and right vertical edges carry continuously routed or applied classical moulding profiles running the full height of the panel: ogee (S-curve convex to concave), cove (concave quarter-round), bead and astragal (small convex half-round), and fillet (flat step transitions between profiles). Moulding cross-section is symmetrical on both edges. Moulding edge width approximately 20–40 mm per edge; moulding projection depth approximately 8–15 mm. The panel reads as a classical pilaster or architectural column strip and is the primary decorative character element of the entire piece. It is a structural element — not applied trim — fixed to the carcass top panel, bottom panel, mid-level shelf, and back panel. This element must be explicitly detailed in all production drawings with a full cross-section profile drawing of the moulded edge at a minimum scale of 1:2.
structural_elements.material: 25 mm solid hardwood preferred (oak, ash, or tulipwood for painted finish) — or 18 mm MDF core with solid hardwood moulding applied and integrated to appear as one continuous piece. Finished identically to the rest of the cabinet throughout.
structural_elements.position: Dead centre of the cabinet at the exact centreline, full height from base plinth to underside of cornice, between the left and right inner display and storage bays

structural_elements.name: Left Inner Display and Storage Bay
structural_elements.description: The wider inner section immediately to the right of the left flanking tower and to the left of the central divider pilaster. Bay width approximately 55–65 cm. Upper zone (above mid-level structural shelf): open display shelving arranged as a dense, grid-like matrix of adjustable shelves and vertical sub-dividers creating individual display niches of varying sizes — approximately 3 horizontal shelf levels with 2–3 vertical sub-divisions per level, consistent with a traditional dresser hutch or bibliothèque upper section for displaying china, books, and decorative objects. All shelves adjustable on shelf pins; all niches backed by full recessed back panel. Upper zone depth 350–380 mm (stepped back from lower zone). Lower zone (below mid-level structural shelf): bank of 3 drawer fronts in a vertical stack, traditional raised or flat panel style with antique brass bail pulls, on full-extension soft-close undermount slides. Drawer box construction in 12–15 mm birch plywood. Face frame with classical moulding detailing on stiles and rails. Full back panel throughout upper and lower zones.
structural_elements.material: 18 mm MDF or plywood carcass, 25 mm fixed structural shelves, 18 mm adjustable shelves on shelf pins, solid hardwood face frame and drawer fronts, 12–15 mm plywood drawer boxes, 6 mm back panel
structural_elements.position: Second from left, full height, between left flanking tower and central divider pilaster

structural_elements.name: Right Inner Display and Storage Bay
structural_elements.description: Exact mirror image of the left inner display and storage bay. The wider inner section immediately to the left of the right flanking tower and to the right of the central divider pilaster. Bay width approximately 55–65 cm. Upper zone: identical dense grid-like open display shelving matrix with adjustable shelves and vertical sub-dividers creating individual display niches. Lower zone: matching bank of 3 drawer fronts in a vertical stack with antique brass bail pulls on full-extension soft-close slides. Full symmetry confirmed. Identical construction, materials, shelf configuration, and hardware to the left inner bay. Full back panel throughout.
structural_elements.material: 18 mm MDF or plywood carcass, 25 mm fixed structural shelves, 18 mm adjustable shelves on shelf pins, solid hardwood face frame and drawer fronts, 12–15 mm plywood drawer boxes, 6 mm back panel
structural_elements.position: Second from right, full height, between central divider pilaster and right flanking tower

structural_elements.name: Mid-Level Horizontal Structural Shelf and Counter Rail
structural_elements.description: A fixed full-width horizontal structural shelf integrated into the carcass at approximately 85–95 cm from floor level, spanning across both inner display and storage bays and passing behind the central divider pilaster. This element marks the transition between the upper open display zone and the lower drawer storage zone and is the primary horizontal datum of the composition. Constructed in 25 mm solid hardwood or built-up veneered board for structural integrity across the full span. Solid hardwood moulding profile on the front edge. In the recommended stepped-depth configuration, the lower storage zone is 420 mm deep and the upper display zone is 350–380 mm deep, causing this shelf to project as a visible counter ledge of approximately 40–70 mm beyond the face of the upper section — functioning as a display or serving ledge. In a uniform-depth configuration, this shelf is flush with the cabinet face and reads as a fixed shelf with a moulded front edge only. Long spans exceeding 700 mm should be reinforced with concealed steel flat-bar routed into the underside. This is a structural fixed shelf integrated into the carcass — not a freestanding or removable element.
structural_elements.material: 25 mm solid hardwood or built-up veneered board with solid hardwood front moulding profile; optional concealed steel flat-bar reinforcement routed into underside for spans exceeding 700 mm
structural_elements.position: Full width of both inner display and storage bays and behind the central divider pilaster, at approximately 85–95 cm above finished floor level

structural_elements.name: Fixed Mid-Height Dividing Shelf — Flanking Towers
structural_elements.description: A fixed 25 mm structural horizontal shelf in each flanking outer tower, dividing the upper open shelving zone from the lower cupboard zone. Acts as structural bay divider, visual break, and door-stop reference for the lower cupboard door. Positioned at approximately 90–100 cm from floor to align visually with the mid-level counter rail of the inner bays. Solid hardwood edge banding on the front edge. Painted to match.
structural_elements.material: 25 mm MDF or plywood with solid hardwood front edge banding
structural_elements.position: Mid-height of each flanking outer tower, approximately 90–100 cm from floor, one in each tower

structural_elements.name: Upper Display Shelving Matrix — Inner Bays
structural_elements.description: The dense, grid-like open display shelving system occupying the upper zone of both inner display and storage bays above the mid-level structural shelf. Comprises a matrix of adjustable horizontal shelves on shelf pins in 5 mm holes on 32 mm centres, combined with fixed or adjustable vertical sub-dividers creating individual display niches and cubbies of varying sizes. Approximately 3 horizontal shelf levels with 2–3 vertical sub-divisions per level per bay. All shelves and sub-dividers in 18 mm MDF or plywood with solid hardwood front lipping. All niches backed by full recessed 6 mm back panel. Shelf depth 350–380 mm in stepped-depth configuration. This shelving matrix is the primary display zone of the piece and should read as a formal but richly subdivided niche arrangement.
structural_elements.material: 18 mm MDF or plywood shelves and sub-dividers with solid hardwood front lipping, on adjustable shelf pins; 6 mm back panel
structural_elements.position: Upper zone of left and right inner bays, above mid-level structural shelf, between mid-level shelf and underside of cornice

structural_elements.name: Lower Drawer Bank — Inner Bays
structural_elements.description: A symmetrical bank of six drawer fronts in total across the two inner display and storage bays — three drawer fronts per bay arranged as a vertical stack. Drawer fronts in traditional raised or flat panel solid hardwood style with antique brass bail pulls or cup pulls. Drawer boxes in 12–15 mm birch plywood on full-extension soft-close undermount slides. Drawer fronts fitted with traditional face frame overlay. All drawers backed by full recessed back panel. The six-drawer arrangement is the primary lower storage element of the piece.
structural_elements.material: Solid hardwood drawer fronts in raised or flat panel style; 12–15 mm birch plywood drawer boxes; full-extension soft-close undermount slides; antique brass bail pulls or cup pulls
structural_elements.position: Lower zone of left and right inner bays, below mid-level structural shelf, above base plinth

spatial_layout: The cabinet is arranged as a symmetrical five-part built-in elevation against a single straight wall. Reading left to right: left outer flanking tower (full height, narrow, upper open shelving over lower single-door cupboard), left inner display and storage bay (full height, wider, upper dense niche shelving matrix over lower three-drawer bank), central full-height divider pilaster with classical moulded edges (the defining design element at the exact centreline), right inner display and storage bay (mirror of left inner bay, upper dense niche shelving over lower three-drawer bank), right outer flanking tower (mirror of left tower, upper open shelving over lower single-door cupboard). The continuous classical crown and cornice runs the full width at the top. The continuous base plinth runs the full width at floor level. A fixed mid-level horizontal structural shelf and counter rail at approximately 85–95 cm above floor creates a strong horizontal datum across the full width of the inner bays, visually separating the upper display zone from the lower storage zone and functioning as a projecting counter ledge in the recommended stepped-depth configuration. The upper inner bays are dominated by a dense, grid-like matrix of open display niches. The lower inner bays contain the six-drawer bank. The lower outer tower bays contain single hinged panel doors. The overall composition is strongly horizontal and architectural, reading as formal classical fitted joinery integrated into the wall. Standard upper display shelf depth approximately 350–380 mm; lower storage depth approximately 400–420 mm. Any horizontal shelf span exceeding 600 mm is thickened to 25 mm, lipped in solid hardwood, sub-divided, or reinforced with concealed steel flat-bar. Minimum front clearance of approximately 900 mm required for drawer extension and door swing. The piece is fixed mechanically to wall studs or masonry and to the floor through the base plinth.

context.placement: Built into a living room, dining room, study, hallway, or formal reception room along a single straight wall — designed as a permanent fitted installation
context.surroundings: Designed to read as integrated architectural millwork. Fitted between adjacent walls or within a wall recess. Skirting boards coordinated into or returned against the base plinth. Crown moulding either scribed to the ceiling or soffit condition or a small painted infill panel used above if ceiling height exceeds cabinet height. Back panels fixed to wall. All gaps between cabinet and adjacent walls, ceiling, and floor to be scribed, caulked, and painted for a fully integrated built-in appearance.
context.scale_reference: Sized as a statement built-in for domestic interiors with ceiling heights of approximately 240–270 cm. Overall dimensions 220 cm wide by 230 cm tall by 420 mm deep (lower zone) and 350–380 mm deep (upper inner display zone). Minimum front clearance of approximately 900 mm required for comfortable use.

views_to_generate.label: Floor Plan — Built-In Cabinet
views_to_generate.description: Top-down plan view at 1:20 scale showing the full 220 cm width and overall depth footprint of the cabinet. Show the five-part bay arrangement with bay widths dimensioned: left outer tower, left inner bay, central divider pilaster (with moulded edges indicated), right inner bay, right outer tower. Show the stepped-depth relationship between lower storage zone (420 mm deep) and upper inner display zone (350–380 mm deep). Show crown cornice projection at top of plan. Show base plinth setback at bottom of plan. Indicate wall fixing line behind cabinet. Dimension all bay widths, overall width, lower depth, upper depth, and crown projection depth. Include north arrow or orientation indicator.

views_to_generate.label: Front Elevation — Main Cabinet Face
views_to_generate.description: Primary front elevation at 1:20 scale showing the complete five-part symmetrical composition across the full 220 cm width and 230 cm height. Show from left to right: left outer flanking tower with upper open shelving and lower single hinged raised-panel door; left inner display and storage bay with upper dense grid-like niche shelving matrix (show approximately 3 horizontal shelf levels with 2–3 vertical sub-divisions per level) and lower three-drawer bank with bail pulls; central full-height divider pilaster at exact centreline with classical moulded edges indicated by profile lines on both vertical edges (ogee, cove, bead, fillet); right inner display and storage bay mirroring left inner bay; right outer flanking tower mirroring left tower. Show continuous classical crown and cornice at top with stepped profile. Show continuous base plinth at bottom. Show mid-level horizontal structural shelf and counter rail as a strong horizontal datum at approximately 85–95 cm from floor across the full inner bay width. Show all drawer fronts with bail pull hardware. Show all lower tower cupboard doors with cup pull or knob hardware. Dimension overall width, overall height, cornice height, plinth height, mid-level shelf height, tower widths, inner bay widths, and central divider width. Include a detail callout circle on the central divider pilaster edge indicating the classical moulding profile cross-section to be drawn at 1:2 in a separate detail.

generation_notes: CRITICAL DESIGN REQUIREMENT: The single most important element of this piece is the full-height central vertical divider pilaster with continuously routed classical moulding profiles (ogee, cove, bead and astragal, fillet) along both vertical edges running the full height from base plinth to underside of cornice. This element must be explicit, prominent, and correctly detailed in all drawings and images. A full cross-section profile drawing of the moulded edge must be produced at minimum 1:2 scale. Do not omit or simplify this element. CONSTRUCTION STANDARDS: Use 18 mm stock for general carcass construction throughout. Use 25 mm stock for all visually important or structurally loaded members — central divider pilaster, mid-level structural shelf and counter rail, long-span display shelves, and tower dividing shelves. All exposed board edges must be lipped in solid hardwood or high-quality veneered edging. All sections must have full recessed back panels in 6 mm veneered plywood or 3 mm HDF. Reinforce all horizontal shelf spans exceeding 700 mm with thicker stock, hardwood lipping, intermediate vertical sub-divisions, or concealed steel flat-bar reinforcement routed into the underside. INSTALLATION: Anchor the cabinet securely to wall studs or masonry through the back panel and through the base plinth to the floor. Protect the plinth and floor junction against moisture with sealant. Scribe or caulk all gaps between the cabinet and adjacent walls, ceiling, and floor for a fully integrated built-in appearance. If installed to the ceiling, scribe the crown into the soffit or use a small painted infill panel above. FINISH: Painted off-white or warm cream satin enamel is the primary finish specification. All elements — carcass, face frame, mouldings, drawer fronts, door panels, central divider — must be finished identically for a fully unified appearance. Interior display shelving backs may optionally be painted a slightly deeper tone or lined with fabric or wallpaper. HARDWARE: All hardware must be consistent in antique brass or aged bronze finish throughout. Traditional bail pulls or cup pulls on all drawer fronts. Cup pulls or knobs on lower tower cupboard doors. Concealed soft-close cup hinges on all doors. Full-extension soft-close undermount slides on all drawers. Adjustable shelf pins in 5 mm holes on 32 mm centres for all adjustable shelves. UNRESOLVED CLIENT DECISION POINTS requiring confirmation before production: (1) Confirm final overall width — 220 cm per this specification or wider per sketch proportions (260–320 cm). (2) Confirm stepped-depth configuration (recommended) versus uniform depth throughout. (3) Confirm painted finish versus stained natural wood finish. (4) Confirm hardware style — traditional bail pulls versus cup pulls versus push-to-open. (5) Confirm lower tower door detail — solid raised panel versus glazed. (6) Confirm whether an upper central door pair is required — this element is suggested by some sketch readings but is not confirmed by the text brief and has been omitted from this specification pending client direction. If confirmed, treat as a probable pair of timber-framed solid or glazed doors in the upper central zone adjacent to the divider, requiring revision of the upper inner bay shelving layout. DIMENSIONAL CONFLICT NOTE: The sketch's five-bay proportional composition implies a cabinet width of approximately 260–320 cm if drawn to realistic scale. The text brief constrains width to 180–220 cm. The text brief takes precedence in this specification. Client confirmation is required if sketch proportions are intended to be dimensionally accurate.
